lines.speaker: CITIZENS
lines.text: We will be satisfied; let us be satisfied.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Then follow me, and give me audience, friends.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Cassius, go you into the other street,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And part the numbers.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Those that will hear me speak, let ’em stay here;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Those that will follow Cassius, go with him;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And public reasons shall be rendered

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Of Caesar’s death.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: I will hear Brutus speak.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: I will hear Cassius; and compare their reasons,

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: When severally we hear them rendered.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: The noble Brutus is ascended: silence!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Be patient till the last. Romans, countrymen, and lovers! hear me for

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: my cause, and be silent, that you may hear: believe me for

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: mine honour, and have respect to mine honour, that you may believe:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: censure me in your wisdom, and awake your senses, that you may

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: the better judge. If there be any in this assembly, any dear

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: friend of Caesar’s, to him I say, that Brutus’ love to Caesar

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: was no less than his. If then that friend demand why Brutus

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: rose against Caesar, this is my answer:—Not that I loved Caesar less,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: but that I loved Rome more. Had you rather Caesar were living

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: and die all slaves, than that Caesar were dead, to live all

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: free men? As Caesar loved me, I weep for him; as he

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: was fortunate, I rejoice at it; as he was valiant, I honour

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: him: but, as he was ambitious, I slew him. There is tears

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: for his love; joy for his fortune; honour for his valour; and

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: death for his ambition. Who is here so base that would be

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: a bondman? If any, speak; for him have I offended. Who is

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: here so rude that would not be a Roman? If any, speak;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: for him have I offended. Who is here so vile that will

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: not love his country? If any, speak; for him have I offended.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I pause for a reply.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: None, Brutus, none.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Then none have I offended. I have done no more to Caesar

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: than you shall do to Brutus. The question of his death is

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: enrolled in the Capitol; his glory not extenuated, wherein he was worthy,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: nor his offences enforced, for which he suffered death.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Here comes his body, mourned by Mark Antony: who, though he had

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: no hand in his death, shall receive the benefit of his dying,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: a place in the commonwealth; as which of you shall not? With

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: this I depart—that, as I slew my best lover for the good

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: of Rome, I have the same dagger for myself, when it shall

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: please my country to need my death.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Live, Brutus! live, live!

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Bring him with triumph home unto his house.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Give him a statue with his ancestors.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Let him be Caesar.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Caesar’s better parts

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Shall be crown’d in Brutus.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: We’ll bring him to his house

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: With shouts and clamours.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: My countrymen—

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Peace, silence! Brutus speaks.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Peace, ho!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good countrymen, let me depart alone,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And, for my sake, stay here with Antony:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Do grace to Caesar’s corpse, and grace his speech

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Tending to Caesar’s glories; which Mark Antony,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By our permission, is allow’d to make.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I do entreat you, not a man depart,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Save I alone, till Antony have spoke.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Stay, ho! and let us hear Mark Antony.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Let him go up into the public chair;

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: We’ll hear him. Noble Antony, go up.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: For Brutus’ sake, I am beholding to you. Goes into the pulpit.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: What does he say of Brutus?

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: He says, for Brutus’ sake,

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: He finds himself beholding to us all.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: ’Twere best he speak no harm of Brutus here.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: This Caesar was a tyrant.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Nay, that’s certain:

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: We are blest that Rome is rid of him.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Peace! let us hear what Antony can say.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You gentle Romans—

lines.speaker: CITIZENS
lines.text: Peace, ho! let us hear him.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The evil that men do lives after them;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The good is oft interred with their bones;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: So let it be with Caesar. The noble Brutus

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Hath told you Caesar was ambitious:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: If it were so, it was a grievous fault,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And grievously hath Caesar answer’d it.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Here, under leave of Brutus and the rest—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: For Brutus is an honourable man;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: So are they all, all honourable men—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Come I to speak in Caesar’s funeral.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He was my friend, faithful and just to me:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But Brutus says he was ambitious;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And Brutus is an honourable man.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He hath brought many captives home to Rome,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Whose ransoms did the general coffers fill:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Did this in Caesar seem ambitious?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: When that the poor have cried, Caesar hath wept:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Ambition should be made of sterner stuff:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Yet Brutus says he was ambitious;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And Brutus is an honourable man.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You all did see that on the Lupercal

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I thrice presented him a kingly crown,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Which he did thrice refuse: was this ambition?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Yet Brutus says he was ambitious;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And, sure, he is an honourable man.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I speak not to disprove what Brutus spoke,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But here I am to speak what I do know.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You all did love him once, not without cause:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: What cause withholds you then, to mourn for him?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O judgment! thou art fled to brutish beasts,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And men have lost their reason. Bear with me;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: My heart is in the coffin there with Caesar,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And I must pause till it come back to me.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Methinks there is much reason in his sayings.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: If thou consider rightly of the matter,

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Caesar has had great wrong.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Has he, masters?

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: I fear there will a worse come in his place.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Mark’d ye his words? He would not take the crown;

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Therefore ’tis certain he was not ambitious.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: If it be found so, some will dear abide it.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Poor soul! his eyes are red as fire with weeping.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: There’s not a nobler man in Rome than Antony.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Now mark him, he begins again to speak.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But yesterday the word of Caesar might

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Have stood against the world; now lies he there,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And none so poor to do him reverence.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O masters, if I were disposed to stir

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Your hearts and minds to mutiny and rage,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I should do Brutus wrong, and Cassius wrong,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Who, you all know, are honourable men:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I will not do them wrong; I rather choose

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To wrong the dead, to wrong myself and you,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Than I will wrong such honourable men.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But here’s a parchment with the seal of Caesar;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I found it in his closet, ’tis his will:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Let but the commons hear this testament—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Which, pardon me, I do not mean to read—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And they would go and kiss dead Caesar’s wounds

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And dip their napkins in his sacred blood,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Yea, beg a hair of him for memory,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And, dying, mention it within their wills,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Bequeathing it as a rich legacy

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Unto their issue.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: We’ll hear the will: read it, Mark Antony.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: The will, the will! we will hear Caesar’s will.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Have patience, gentle friends, I must not read it;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: It is not meet you know how Caesar loved you.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You are not wood, you are not stones, but men;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And, being men, bearing the will of Caesar,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: It will inflame you, it will make you mad:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: ’Tis good you know not that you are his heirs;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: For, if you should, O, what would come of it!

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Read the will; we’ll hear it, Antony;

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: You shall read us the will, Caesar’s will.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Will you be patient? will you stay awhile?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I have o’ershot myself to tell you of it:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I fear I wrong the honourable men

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Whose daggers have stabb’d Caesar; I do fear it.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: They were traitors: honourable men!

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: The will! the testament!

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: They were villains, murderers: the will! read the will.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You will compel me, then, to read the will?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Then make a ring about the corpse of Caesar,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And let me show you him that made the will.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Shall I descend? and will you give me leave?

lines.speaker: SEVERAL CITIZENS
lines.text: Come down.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Descend.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: You shall have leave. Antony comes down.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: A ring; stand round.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Stand from the hearse, stand from the body.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Room for Antony, most noble Antony.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Nay, press not so upon me; stand far off.

lines.speaker: SEVERAL CITIZENS
lines.text: Stand back; room; bear back.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: If you have tears, prepare to shed them now.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You all do know this mantle: I remember

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The first time ever Caesar put it on;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: ’Twas on a summer’s evening, in his tent,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That day he overcame the Nervii:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Look, in this place ran Cassius’ dagger through:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: See what a rent the envious Casca made:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Through this the well-beloved Brutus stabb’d;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And as he pluck’d his cursed steel away,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Mark how the blood of Caesar follow’d it,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: As rushing out of doors, to be resolved

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: If Brutus so unkindly knock’d, or no;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: For Brutus, as you know, was Caesar’s angel:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Judge, O you gods, how dearly Caesar loved him!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: This was the most unkindest cut of all;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: For when the noble Caesar saw him stab,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Ingratitude, more strong than traitors’ arms,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Quite vanquish’d him: then burst his mighty heart;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And, in his mantle muffling up his face,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Even at the base of Pompey’s statua,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Which all the while ran blood, great Caesar fell.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O, what a fall was there, my countrymen!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Then I, and you, and all of us fell down,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Whilst bloody treason flourish’d over us.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O, now you weep; and, I perceive, you feel

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The dint of pity: these are gracious drops.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Kind souls, what, weep you when you but behold

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Our Caesar’s vesture wounded? Look you here,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Here is himself, marr’d, as you see, with traitors.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: O piteous spectacle!

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: O noble Caesar!

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: O woeful day!

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: O traitors, villains!

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: O most bloody sight!

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: We will be revenged.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Revenge! About! Seek! Burn! Fire! Kill! Slay! Let not a traitor live!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Stay, countrymen.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Peace there! hear the noble Antony.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: We’ll hear him, we’ll follow him, we’ll die with him.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Good friends, sweet friends, let me not stir you up

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To such a sudden flood of mutiny.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: They that have done this deed are honourable:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: What private griefs they have, alas, I know not,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That made them do it: they are wise and honourable,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And will, no doubt, with reasons answer you.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I come not, friends, to steal away your hearts:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I am no orator, as Brutus is;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But, as you know me all, a plain blunt man,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That love my friend; and that they know full well

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That gave me public leave to speak of him:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: For I have neither wit, nor words, nor worth,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Action, nor utterance, nor the power of speech,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To stir men’s blood: I only speak right on;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I tell you that which you yourselves do know;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Show you sweet Caesar’s wounds, poor poor dumb mouths,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And bid them speak for me: but were I Brutus,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And Brutus Antony, there were an Antony

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Would ruffle up your spirits and put a tongue

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: In every wound of Caesar that should move

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The stones of Rome to rise and mutiny.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: We’ll mutiny.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: We’ll burn the house of Brutus.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Away, then! come, seek the conspirators.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Yet hear me, countrymen; yet hear me speak.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Peace, ho! Hear Antony. Most noble Antony!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Why, friends, you go to do you know not what:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Wherein hath Caesar thus deserved your loves?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Alas, you know not: I must tell you then:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You have forgot the will I told you of.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Most true. The will! Let’s stay and hear the will.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Here is the will, and under Caesar’s seal.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To every Roman citizen he gives,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To every several man, seventy-five drachmas.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Most noble Caesar! We’ll revenge his death.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: O royal Caesar!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Hear me with patience.

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Peace, ho!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Moreover, he hath left you all his walks,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: His private arbours and new-planted orchards,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: On this side Tiber; he hath left them you,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And to your heirs forever, common pleasures,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To walk abroad, and recreate yourselves.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Here was a Caesar! when comes such another?

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Never, never. Come, away, away!

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: We’ll burn his body in the holy place,

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: And with the brands fire the traitors’ houses.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Take up the body.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Go fetch fire.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Pluck down benches.

lines.speaker: FOURTH CITIZEN
lines.text: Pluck down forms, windows, anything.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Now let it work. Mischief, thou art afoot,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Take thou what course thou wilt!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: How now, fellow!

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Sir, Octavius is already come to Rome.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Where is he?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: He and Lepidus are at Caesar’s house.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And thither will I straight to visit him:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He comes upon a wish. Fortune is merry,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And in this mood will give us anything.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: I heard him say, Brutus and Cassius

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Are rid like madmen through the gates of Rome.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Belike they had some notice of the people,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: How I had moved them. Bring me to Octavius.

stage_directions.text: Enter Brutus and Cassius, and a throng of Citizens.
Exit Cassius, with some of the Citizens. Brutus goes into the pulpit.
Enter Antony and others, with Caesar’s body.

glosses.definition: divide the crowd into two parts (one to each speaker)
separately, one at a time, we hear
Brutus has climbed up (onto the Rostra, the speaker's platform)

dear friends; devoted allies. In Elizabethan usage 'lover' frequently means friend or well-wisher — not romantic. Brutus's 'Romans, countrymen, and lovers' is emphatically NOT the modern sense; modern readers who hear it as incongruous or comic have misread the word
judge me (not modern 'criticise')

glosses.phrase: Brutus' love to Caesar / was no less than his
glosses.definition: my love for Caesar was no less than his friend's — Brutus's rhetorical premise

glosses.phrase: Not that I loved Caesar less, but that I loved Rome more
glosses.definition: Brutus's most-quoted line; the perfect Stoic-republican formula that attempts to make assassination an act of higher love
slave; bondsman

Brutus makes 'ambition' the sole justification; in Elizabethan political vocabulary 'ambition' was a specific vice — ungoverned desire for authority — heavier in weight than modern 'ambition'. The charge is more damning than it sounds to a modern ear
barbarous; uncivilised
formally recorded in the public archives
lessened; diminished — his virtues have not been downplayed
exaggerated; pressed hard — his faults have not been overstated
dearest friend — again the Elizabethan sense, not romantic
bring him home in triumph — the Citizens propose for Brutus the very honour the tribunes rebuked for Caesar in 1.1

the crowd's spontaneous collapse of principle — Brutus has just killed a man for being on the road to Caesarhood; they immediately propose Brutus as Caesar. Shakespeare's sharp moment on democratic volatility

glosses.phrase: Caesar's better parts / Shall be crown'd in Brutus
glosses.definition: Caesar's good qualities shall be crowned in Brutus — the crowd simply transfers the crown-impulse
obliged; indebted (older form of 'beholden')
the First Citizen's firm conviction at this moment — which Antony's speech will reverse

glosses.phrase: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears
glosses.definition: Antony's famous opening — 'lend me your ears' is idiom for 'give me your attention.' In context this is strategic manipulation, not sincere address: Antony is asking permission to speak from a crowd that already hates him, establishing false humility before systematically dismantling Brutus's justification

glosses.phrase: bury Caesar, not to praise him
glosses.definition: Antony's opening disclaimer — which the entire rest of the speech contradicts. The structural irony of denying he is making a speech while making the greatest speech in the play
buried with the dead man's bones; forgotten with the corpse

glosses.phrase: Brutus / Hath told you Caesar was ambitious
glosses.definition: Antony sets up his refrain; 'ambitious' will be the word he dismantles
serious failing

Antony's refrain. Each of the six repetitions lowers the word's credit; by the end 'honourable' has become an accusation of hypocrisy. Shakespeare shows how repetition, stripped of tonal sincerity, can reverse a word's meaning
the public treasury

glosses.phrase: Ambition should be made of sterner stuff
glosses.definition: Antony's pivot — real ambition is tougher than this; if Caesar wept at the poor, he was no ambitious tyrant
the Lupercalian festival of 1.2, where Antony offered Caesar the crown three times

glosses.phrase: thrice presented him a kingly crown
glosses.definition: three times offered him a crown — Casca's report in 1.2 restaged as evidence of Caesar's moderation

glosses.phrase: O judgment! thou art fled to brutish beasts
glosses.definition: judgment has fled to wild animals — men have lost their reason

glosses.phrase: must pause till it come back to me
glosses.definition: Antony's calculated theatrical pause — false emotion as persuasive device

glosses.phrase: parchment with the seal of Caesar
glosses.definition: a document under Caesar's official seal — his will
private study
last will and testament

glosses.phrase: dip their napkins in his sacred blood
glosses.definition: dip handkerchiefs in his blood — the relic-hunting of martyrs' blood, transferred from Christian practice onto Caesar

glosses.phrase: Bequeathing it as a rich legacy
glosses.definition: leaving it in their own wills as a treasure
fitting; proper
overreached; spoken more than I meant to
circle around the body
a cloak; Caesar's outer garment, worn over the toga — bloodied and torn by the daggers. The mantle becomes Antony's physical prop for grief
defeated the Nervii — a Belgic tribe Caesar conquered in 57 BCE; detail from Plutarch, though the 'mantle' anecdote is Shakespeare's invention
Antony's fiction — he cannot know whose dagger made which cut, but the naming makes the wounds personal
malicious Casca; the striker of the first blow
accursed blade
knocked in an unnatural way — pun on kin/kind; betrayal of family-blood feeling
Caesar's beloved — Brutus as Caesar's guardian spirit or dearest one
the cruellest cut — from the 'unkind' (unnatural/unkinned) Brutus; the line enters the language
covering his face with the cloak — Plutarch's detail; Caesar shrouded himself as he fell
Pompey's statue — Caesar fell beneath the statue of his defeated rival; Plutarch notes the fit of revenge
bloody betrayal triumphed over us
the stroke/stamp of pity; pity's mark
garment; clothing
rebellion; uprising (against authority)

glosses.phrase: I am no orator, as Brutus is
glosses.definition: Antony's crowning false humility — while delivering what will become the most-taught example of public rhetoric in English
Antony's pose of rustic directness; the opposite of what he is

glosses.phrase: neither wit, nor words, nor worth
glosses.definition: no intelligence, no words, no value — a fisherman's disclaimer from a master rhetorician
speak straightforwardly (yet another false claim)
stir your emotions; rouse you

glosses.phrase: stones of Rome to rise and mutiny
glosses.definition: the very stones of Rome would rebel — the classical trope of hyperbolic civic outrage
a drachma was a Greek silver coin; roughly a Roman denarius; 75 denarii represented several weeks' labour — a substantial individual bequest
enclosed gardens and groves
on the Roman (east) bank of the Tiber — Caesar's pleasure-grounds willed to the citizens
refresh yourselves; take recreation
burning sticks; firebrands
benches; long seats
mischief is now loose and moving — Antony's grim satisfaction as the mob runs riot
Fortune (the goddess of chance) is in a generous mood
probably; it seems likely

annotations.title: Brutus in prose: the oration that tries to be a philosophical proof
annotations.body:
Brutus speaks his funeral oration in prose. This is the play's single most important formal decision. In Elizabethan dramatic convention, prose carries one of several meanings — the low-class, the mad, the comic, the deliberative, the philosophical. In Brutus's case the choice signals rational sincerity: he is offering his reasons, not his passions. The oration is constructed as a series of balanced antitheses ('Not that I loved Caesar less, but that I loved Rome more'), rhetorical questions ('Who is here so base that would be a bondman?'), and conditional self-offerings ('I have done no more to Caesar than you shall do to Brutus').

The structure is formal: it presents itself as a philosophical demonstration rather than a speech. The crowd's response is its first-order test. The citizens do cheer ('Live, Brutus! live, live!') but then, in the play's most unsettling democratic moment, one of them proposes 'Let him be Caesar.' Brutus has killed a man for approaching Caesarhood; the crowd offers the same status to Brutus seconds after the speech. Shakespeare is showing us something uncomfortable about the audience's real posture: they cheer the speaker, not the argument. The abstraction of Brutus's reasoning was never going to hold against the crowd's appetite for figures to venerate.

Notice too the word 'lovers' (L11). It is the single most load-bearing word in the speech, and modern readers routinely misread it. 'Romans, countrymen, and lovers' does not mean what it would mean in a contemporary novel; 'lover' in Elizabethan usage is primarily 'devoted friend, well-wisher.' Brutus is addressing three categories of civic allegiance — citizens (Romans), native-born fellows (countrymen), and dear friends (lovers). The tonal register of the opening depends on hearing this correctly.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, *The Development of Shakespeare's Imagery* (1951)
Gary Wills, *Rome and Rhetoric: Shakespeare's Julius Caesar* (2011)

annotations.title: Antony in verse: the speech that is taught in every rhetoric class
annotations.body:
Antony's oration is in verse. The formal contrast with Brutus's prose is structural, not decorative. Where Brutus signals rational deliberation, Antony signals elevated passion. But the verse itself does specific technical work that makes this the most-studied political speech in English.

*The 'honourable' refrain.* Antony repeats 'honourable' six times in the speech's first movement (L75, 80, 86, 91, 116, 120). Each repetition is tonally more ironic than the last. By the fifth repetition, the word has been drained of its normal sincerity and become an accusation: Brutus cannot possibly be honourable if Caesar was not ambitious, and Caesar was, by each of the examples Antony supplies, not ambitious. The repetition is the argument. Shakespeare has shown us how a word, stripped of tonal confirmation, inverts.

*The 'I am no orator, as Brutus is' trick.* While delivering one of the highest examples of English oratory ever composed, Antony claims not to be an orator. The paradox is rhetorical preterition — claiming not to say what you are in fact saying — and it protects Antony from the charge of manipulation even as he manipulates. Shakespeare lets the audience see the technique working: the Fourth Citizen's 'I fear there will a worse come in his place' (L104) is the exact response Antony wants, arrived at through a speech he is denying he is giving.

*The physical props.* The will, the mantle, the body — each is produced at a calculated moment. The mantle carries specific wounds Antony attributes to specific daggers ('In this place ran Cassius' dagger through') even though Antony cannot actually know whose dagger made which cut. The attribution is fiction in service of concreteness. The body at the climax is simply shown: 'Here is himself, marr'd, as you see, with traitors.' Prose-Brutus gave them reasons; verse-Antony gives them an object to grieve.

*The will.* Withheld, demanded, withheld again, finally read. The technique is classic preterition plus delay — making the audience co-author the speech's most incendiary material by begging for it. When the will is finally read (L225–234), the 75 drachmas and the public walks are anticlimactic; the real effect was the crowd's insistence on hearing it. Antony has taught them to demand their own inflammation.

*The crowd's volatility.* The First Citizen begins with 'This Caesar was a tyrant' (L62); by L191 he is shouting 'Revenge! About! Seek! Burn! Fire! Kill! Slay!' In eleven minutes of stage-time Shakespeare has reversed the polity. The reversal is a warning about democratic rhetoric that is uncomfortable for democratic readers and was (some scholars argue) a specific warning to Shakespeare's Elizabethan audience about the fragility of a polity built on the passions of crowds.
sources: Quintilian, *Institutio Oratoria* IX.iii (on figures of repetition)
Stanley Fish, 'Rhetoric', in *Critical Terms for Literary Study* (1995)

annotations.title: 'Lovers' — the one-word gloss that keeps the opening from being comic
annotations.body:
Brutus's 'Romans, countrymen, and lovers!' is the phrase modern readers most often mishear. In Elizabethan usage 'lover' means primarily a devoted friend, a well-wisher, a trusted ally — not, except secondarily, a sexual partner. 'Lover' is how Artemidorus signs his warning letter in 2.3 ('Thy lover, Artemidorus') — the Greek teacher is not declaring romantic attachment to Caesar; he is declaring loyalty.

Shakespeare uses the word this way throughout the play. Cassius in 5.1 says 'Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age'; Brutus at 3.2 L38 calls Caesar 'my best lover' meaning his closest friend. The OED documents this sense (lover, n. §1.a) as standard from Old English through 1700.

If the reader imports the modern romantic sense, Brutus's opening becomes confusing or comic. If the reader hears the Elizabethan sense, the tricolon lands: citizens, fellow natives, trusted friends. The single most important gloss for any first-time reader of this speech is this one.
sources: Gordon Williams, *A Glossary of Shakespeare's Sexual Language* (1997)
Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick, *Between Men* (1985) — on friendship/love vocabulary in early modern English

annotations.title: 'Let him be Caesar': the crowd inverts Brutus's argument in one line
annotations.body:
Three citizens respond to Brutus's speech by proposing, in sequence, that Brutus be brought home in triumph, given a statue, and — 'Let him be Caesar.' The Fourth Citizen adds: 'Caesar's better parts / Shall be crown'd in Brutus.'

Brutus has just finished killing Caesar to prevent any one man from being crowned Caesar. The crowd immediately offers Brutus the crown. Shakespeare is making a specific argument here — one uncomfortable for democratic readers and sharp for Elizabethan ones. The Caesar-impulse in Rome is not about Caesar; it is about the crowd's appetite for a single figure to venerate. Brutus's speech has not instructed the crowd in republican principle; it has simply introduced a new candidate. The rhetorical success of the oration is, in political terms, its failure.

This is where the play's political pessimism lives most clearly. Antony has not yet spoken; the reversal that the crowd will undergo is thus not solely Antony's work. Shakespeare has shown us, before Antony begins, that the crowd was always ready to transfer its veneration rather than to reject the category of veneration itself. Antony does not create the appetite; he feeds it.
sources: Annabel Patterson, *Shakespeare and the Popular Voice* (1989)

annotations.title: 'Honourable' said six times: rhetoric as tonal erosion
annotations.body:
Antony's refrain 'Brutus is an honourable man' occurs at lines 75, 80, 86, 91, (via 'honourable men' at) 116, and 120 — six times in about 45 lines. Each iteration is separated from the last by new evidence of Caesar's non-ambition: the ransoms, the poor's tears, the thrice-refused crown. The technique is classical: *paralipsis* (pretending to pass over something while stating it) combined with *incrementum* (accumulation of examples).

The effect is tonal erosion. The first 'honourable' is straight; the second carries doubt; by the fourth, the word means its opposite. Shakespeare is showing that the sincerity of language depends on a shared sense of match between tone and content. Strip the match and the word inverts. This is not subtle manipulation — the crowd's 'They were villains, murderers!' (L147) shows exactly when the inversion completes.

Aristotle in the *Rhetoric* (II.21) discusses this as a property of formal argument. Quintilian (IX.iii) formalises it as a figure. Antony's achievement is not invention but application — turning an Athenian figure on a Roman crowd in an English theatre. Shakespeare is writing a rhetorical primer here; the scene has been taught in every humanist curriculum in English for four centuries and shows no signs of losing its position.
sources: Richard Lanham, *A Handlist of Rhetorical Terms* (1991)

annotations.title: The mantle, the wounds, the body: concrete objects for abstract grief
annotations.body:
Antony's second movement is built around three physical objects: Caesar's mantle (the bloodied cloak), the individual wounds, and finally the corpse itself. Where Brutus's prose was built from abstractions (ambition, honour, Rome, love), Antony's verse is built from specific things the audience can see.

The technique is epistemological. Abstract argument requires agreement on terms; concrete display requires only seeing. Antony shows the mantle and names Cassius's dagger-hole in it; he invites the crowd to 'behold / Our Caesar's vesture wounded' (L185–186); he finally pulls back the mantle and displays 'himself, marr'd, as you see, with traitors.' The crowd responds to this display, not to an argument. The first citizen's sequence — 'O piteous spectacle!' 'O noble Caesar!' 'O woeful day!' 'O traitors, villains!' — is not a reasoned response but a participation in display.

Shakespeare's implicit argument is that mass political persuasion is primarily visual. The funeral oration's verbal technique is the frame; the concrete display of wounds is the core. Modern political rhetoric has not moved on from this. The American advertising industry's discovery of photography in the 1950s is the same discovery: an abstract case for war, for a product, for a politician is enormously amplified by physical display. Antony in 3.2 is the West's first teacher of this.

annotations.title: 'I am no orator' — preterition at its highest pitch
annotations.body:
Antony's disavowal of oratorical skill, delivered at the speech's emotional peak, is the single most studied instance of rhetorical preterition in English. Preterition is the figure in which the speaker denies saying what they are saying; its effect is to shelter the speaker from responsibility for the utterance while still producing it. 'I am no orator, as Brutus is; / But, as you know me all, a plain blunt man' is textbook.

The paradox works because the audience colludes in it. Having spent fifty lines being manipulated by a master, the crowd chooses to receive Antony's self-description as rough plain-speaking. Note the specific inversion Antony performs: Brutus, who was actually trying to argue from reasons, is tagged as 'orator'; Antony, who is manipulating the crowd with every device in classical rhetoric, is tagged as 'plain blunt man.' The inversion protects Antony from being examined for technique.

Shakespeare's placement of this moment matters. It comes after the mantle-and-wounds display (the visual appeal) and before the will (the transactional appeal). The 'plain blunt man' pose is the hinge between the two. It makes the crowd feel that what they have just seen was not rhetoric but confession, and thus primes them to receive the next, transactional move as simple generosity. Every part of the speech reinforces the others; preterition is the technique that makes the package look not like rhetoric but like life.

trials.prompt: Brutus's oration is in prose; Antony's is in verse. The formal contrast signals:
options: Two rhetorical stances — Brutus's rational-philosophical (prose was the register of the learned and the deliberative); Antony's elevated-passionate (verse was the register of tragic heightening)

trials.prompt: 'Romans, countrymen, and lovers' — what does 'lovers' mean here?
options: Devoted friends, well-wishers — the primary Elizabethan sense of 'lover' (not romantic); the same usage Artemidorus makes in 2.3

trials.prompt: After Brutus's speech, the crowd proposes 'Let him be Caesar.' What does this reveal?
options: That the Caesar-impulse in Rome is not about Caesar specifically — it is about the crowd's appetite for a single figure to venerate; Brutus's speech has supplied a new candidate rather than disabled the category
That the crowd is loyal to Brutus
That the crowd wants more speeches

trials.prompt: Antony repeats 'honourable' six times in his speech's first movement. The effect is:
options: Tonal erosion — stripped of matching tone, the word inverts; by the fifth repetition 'honourable' has become an accusation

trials.prompt: 'I am no orator, as Brutus is' is an example of:
options: Rhetorical preterition — the figure in which the speaker denies saying what they are saying; delivered at the emotional peak of a masterly speech, it inverts the actual roles (Brutus, who argued, is the 'orator'; Antony, who manipulates, is the 'plain blunt man')

trials.prompt: Why does Antony withhold and then reveal the will?
options: To make the crowd co-author their own inflammation — by insisting on hearing the will, they feel they chose the incendiary material; the 75-drachma bequest is anticlimax, the demand to hear it was the real effect

trials.prompt: Why is 3.2 taught in every rhetoric class in the English-speaking world?
options: Because it demonstrates, in one scene, the full technical apparatus of mass political persuasion — formal register (prose/verse), ironic refrain, preterition, concrete display, transactional reveal — all in active use on a volatile crowd

trials.prompt: Shakespeare's argument about democratic rhetoric in this scene is:
options: Optimistic — the crowd ultimately reaches the right conclusion
Pessimistic and uncomfortable — the crowd is volatile, its veneration transferable, and a sufficiently skilled rhetorician can reverse its politics in the space of one speech
That Brutus is the better speaker

trials.prompt: What specific claim is Antony making when he attributes particular wounds to particular daggers ('In this place ran Cassius' dagger through')?
options: Rhetorical fiction in service of concreteness — Antony cannot in fact know whose dagger made which cut; the named attribution makes abstract betrayal into personal injury
